documents.title: The Past, Present, and Future of eBPF and Its Path to Revolutionizing Systems
documents.description: This blog post mainly references Alexei Starovoitov's presentation "Modernize BPF for the Next 10 Years" at BPFConf 2024.
documents.titleText: the past, present, and future of ebpf and its path to revolutionizing systems
documents.descriptionText: this blog post mainly references alexei starovoitov's presentation "modernize bpf for the next 10 years" at bpfconf 2024. this blog post mainly references alexei starovoitov's presentation "modernize bpf for the next 10 years" at bpfconf 2024.
documents.bodyTerms: this blog post mainly references alexei starovoitov's presentation "modernize bpf for the next 10 years" bpfconf 2024. imagine having swiss army knife your computer's core operations—something that lets you peek inside how data moves, tweak processes fly, and monitor everything real time. that’s exactly what ebpf (extended berkeley packet filter) offers. over past decade, has transformed from simple filtering tool into powerhouse networking, observability, security. so, what’s ebpf? let’s dive its journey, explore where it’s headed ten years, discuss challenges opportunities lie ahead. exploration will help understand shaping future modern systems. decade review: ebpf's journey far did programmable networking begin with back 2014, world was facing some serious limitations. traditional stacks were rigid, making tough implement custom processing logic tailored specific needs. enter ebpf—a game changer allowed developers write small programs running directly kernel whenever network arrived. innovation meant more control handling, leading better performance flexibility without hassle cumbersome drivers. ebpf, could create solutions fit their exact requirements, paving way efficient innovative applications. shift marked beginning customization hand hand. made xdp high speed networking? gained traction significant hurdle emerged: buff structure linux introduced too much overhead, difficult achieve speeds like gbps. while

documents.titleText: bpftime document userspace ebpf runtime for observability, network, gpu & general extensions framework
documents.descriptionText: high-performance userspace ebpf runtime. run ebpf programs with 10x faster uprobe performance, cross-platform support, and no kernel requirements. ![build and test vm](https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/bpftime/actions/workflows/test-vm.yml) ![build and test runtime](https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/bpftime/actions/workflows/test-runtime.yml) ![doi](https://doi.org/10.48
documents.bodyTerms: build and test runtime doi bpftime high performance userspace ebpf general extension framework designed for userspace. enables faster uprobe, usdt, syscall hooks, xdp, more event sources bypassing the kernel utilizing optimized compiler like llvm. key features quick start examples use cases linux plumbers 23 talk slides osdi '25 paper checkout our documents eunomia.dev deepwiki! not vm, it's includes everything run userspace: loader, verifier, helpers, maps, ufunc multiple events such observability, network, policy access control. has backend options support. only, please see llvmbpf. note currently under active development refactoring towards v2. may contain bugs unstable api. with caution. details, check roadmap. we'd love hear your feedback suggestions! feel free open issue contact us. why bpftime? what's design goal? gains achieve better (e.g., via dbi network drivers), configurable, arch supported jit/aot llvm, while maintaining compatibility ebpf. cross platform functionality large ecosystem where unavailable, older alternative operating systems, level permissions are restricted, without changing tool. flexible language innovation innovation, evolving into production that supports very diverse cases. bpftime's modular allows easy integration library adding new program types touching kernel. wishing could enable rapid prototyping exploration features! dynamic binary rewriting programs userspace, attaching them uprobes, tracepoints

documents.title: Introduction
documents.description: eBPF is a revolutionary technology that originated in the Linux kernel, enabling sandboxed programs to run within the operating system's kernel. It is used to safely and efficiently extend the kernel's capabilities witho
documents.titleText: introduction
documents.descriptionText: ebpf is a revolutionary technology that originated in the linux kernel, enabling sandboxed programs to run within the operating system's kernel. it is used to safely and efficiently extend the kernel's capabilities witho ebpf is a revolutionary technology that originated in the linux kernel, enabling sandboxed programs to run within the operating system's kernel. it is used to safely and efficiently extend the kernel's capabilities witho
documents.bodyTerms: yusheng zheng, tong ebpf revolutionary technology that originated the linux kernel, enabling sandboxed programs run within operating system's kernel. used safely and efficiently extend kernel's capabilities without altering its source code loading kernel modules. this blog, are excited introduce new open user space runtime: bpftime further expands ebpf, allowing existing tools applications, such bcc tools, bpftrace, deepflow, etc., non privileged any modifications, while using same libraries toolchains ebpf. not only provides dynamic tracing extension mechanisms like uprobe system call tracepoints, but also offers order magnitude performance improvement over uprobe. moreover, requires manual instrumentation process restarts. supports inter maps through shared memory, being compatible with maps, seamless operations infrastructure. additionally, includes high llvm jit/aot compilers for various architectures, well lightweight jit interpreter x86. data real world examples, will demonstrate how can effective provide insights into future development. hope bring unprecedented flexibility monitoring, analysis, extension. introduced design implementation plumbers 23 conference[2]. ebpf: from (extended berkeley packet filter) has evolved simple network filtering tool versatile level technology. since inception bpf 1990s, significantly enhanced functionality expanded instruction set direct interaction structures. after joining 2014, became powerful bytecode engine, widely security policies, other areas. growing complexity computing

documents.description: 🚧 It's at an early stage and may contain bugs on more platforms and eBPF programs. We are working on to improve the stability and compatibility. It's not suitable for production use now.

documents.descriptionText: 🚧 it's at an early stage and may contain bugs on more platforms and ebpf programs. we are working on to improve the stability and compatibility. it's not suitable for production use now. 🚧 it's at an early stage and may contain bugs on more platforms and ebpf programs. we are working on to improve the stability and compatibility. it's not suitable for production use now.
documents.bodyTerms: it's early stage and may contain bugs more platforms ebpf programs. are working improve the stability compatibility. not suitable for production use now. you find any suggestions, please feel free open issue, thanks! table contents manual uprobe uretprobe syscall tracing run with ld\ preload directly configurations runtime jit enabled disabled kernel verifier control log level controlling path allow external maps set memory size shared bpftime, can build applications using familiar tools like clang libbpf, execute them userspace. instance, malloc program traces calls aggregates counts hash map. refer documents/build test.md how project. get started, libbpf based starts bpftime cli: make example/malloc example load ./example/malloc/malloc another shell, target inside: start ./example/malloc/victim hello malloc! called from pid 250215 continue malloc... also dynamically attach running process: echo 101771 [1] it: sudo need install root inject: "/root/.bpftime/libbpftime agent.so" successfully injected. id: 1 see output original program: ... 1235 pid=247299 calls: 10 pid=247322 alternatively, our sample ebpf, similar output: example/malloc/malloc 1505 pid=30415 1079 pid=30393 203 pid=29882 1076 pid=34809 8 found examples/opensnoop /.bpftime/bpftime ./example/opensnoop/opensnoop [2023 09 0433.891] [info] manager constructed 0433.892] global shm type 0 0433][info][23999] enabling helper groups ffi, kernel, map default comm err 72101 victim 3 test.txt

documents.bodyTerms: table contents examples use cases minimal tracing the system userspace functions with uprobe all syscalls tracepoints bpftrace bpftime trace spdk bpf features demos gpu/cuda/rocm hotpatching applications error injections xdp advanced attach implementation using library nginx ebpf module dpdk run seamlessly only(no runtime, uprobe) see example/minimal for basic demonstrating core features: uprobe: example syscall: syscall override: demonstrates override return change function values usdt minimal: user statically defined (usdt) supports following types programs: uprobe/uretprobe: start end. tracepoints: specific types. usdt: usdt. you may control flow program. documents/available features.md more details. how are executed (preload dynamic attach) most support (or can adapted to) two execution modes: preload process (legacy but simple): loader server preloaded, and target agent preloaded (via start). this deterministic mode works without injection. late normally, then inject into already running trace). useful when cannot restart target, want probes after has initialized. gpu attach, extract ptx from executable (e.g. via cuobjdump ptx) before injection pass extracted directory injected agent, does not need spawn inside process. uprobe, uretprobe tracepoints(currently x86 only) programs group processes malloc: count malloc calls libc pid. demonstrate hashmap. bashreadline: print entered bash commands shells. sslsniff: ssl/tls connections raw traffic data.

documents.title: Write and Run eBPF on GPU with bpftime
documents.description: bpftime provides GPU support through its CUDA/ROCm attachment implementation, enabling eBPF programs to execute within GPU kernels on NVIDIA and AMD GPUs. This brings eBPF's programmability, observability, and customizat
documents.titleText: write and run ebpf on gpu with bpftime
documents.descriptionText: bpftime provides gpu support through its cuda/rocm attachment implementation, enabling ebpf programs to execute within gpu kernels on nvidia and amd gpus. this brings ebpf's programmability, observability, and customizat bpftime provides gpu support through its cuda/rocm attachment implementation, enabling ebpf programs to execute within gpu kernels on nvidia and amd gpus. this brings ebpf's programmability, observability, and customizat
documents.bodyTerms: yusheng zheng=, tong yu=, yiwei yang= bpftime provides gpu support through its cuda/rocm attachment implementation, enabling ebpf programs execute within kernels nvidia and amd gpus. this brings ebpf's programmability, observability, customization capabilities computing workloads, real time profiling, debugging, runtime extension applications without source code modification. note: still experimental. for questions suggestions, open issue contact us. the problem: observability challenges gpus have become dominant accelerators machine learning, scientific computing, high performance but their simt (single instruction, multiple thread) execution model introduces significant extensibility challenges. modern organize thousands threads into warps (typically 32 threads) that lockstep streaming multiprocessors (sms), with launched asynchronously from host. these navigate complex multi level memory hierarchies ranging fast limited per thread registers, shared memory/lds blocks, l1/l2 caches, slower abundant device memory, while contending preemption make kernel difficult interrupt, inspect, extend. architectural complexity creates rich characteristics including warp divergence, coalescing patterns, bank conflicts, occupancy variations directly impact throughput, yet behaviors remain largely opaque traditional tools. understanding optimizing issues like stalls, bottlenecks, inefficient synchronization, suboptimal utilization requires fine grained visibility flow, access inter coordination happening deep inside gpu, along ability dynamically inject custom logic. are what existing tooling struggles provide flexible,

documents.title: bpftime Attach System
documents.description: The attach system in bpftime provides a modular framework for attaching eBPF programs to various event sources in userspace. It supports multiple attachment types including function probes (uprobes), syscall tracing, and
documents.titleText: bpftime attach system
documents.descriptionText: the attach system in bpftime provides a modular framework for attaching ebpf programs to various event sources in userspace. it supports multiple attachment types including function probes (uprobes), syscall tracing, and the attach system in bpftime provides a modular framework for attaching ebpf programs to various event sources in userspace. it supports multiple attachment types including function probes (uprobes), syscall tracing, and
documents.bodyTerms: the attach system bpftime provides modular framework for attaching ebpf programs various event sources userspace. supports multiple attachment types including function probes (uprobes), syscall tracing, and specialized attachments like cuda kernel instrumentation. architecture overview core design principles 1. each type implemented separate module inheriting from base impl 2. unified interface all implementations follow same pattern consistency 3. extensibility new can easily added implementing 4. cross process support points shared across processes using memory component structure attach/ ├── impl/ common functionality frida uprobe instrumentation trace call tracing cuda/gpu simple simplified wrapper └── text segment transformer/ binary rewriting interception class defines that must follow: public: detach virtual int id(int id) 0; create with callback callback( run &&cb, const private data &private data, type) register custom helper functions void helpers( callback); specific function( std::string &name, data); key executes program prepared context configuration override return set thread local modifying values (frida impl) dynamic gum framework. features: entry (uprobe) (uretprobe) (modify behavior) replacement (ureplace) types: (6): execute uretprobe (7): (1008): behavior ureplace (1009): replace entirely components: state capture via regs different probe scenarios cpu management (syscall intercepts traces calls without support. exit per global callbacks minimal performance

documents.title: bpftimetool
documents.description: Command-line tool for inspecting and managing bpftime shared memory state.
documents.titleText: bpftimetool
documents.descriptionText: command-line tool for inspecting and managing bpftime shared memory state. command-line tool for inspecting and managing bpftime shared memory state.
documents.bodyTerms: command line tool for inspecting and managing bpftime shared memory state. overview bpftimetool provides utilities to: export state json inspection backup import ebpf objects from into run programs with performance benchmarking remove segments system wide installation after building bpftime, the available at: /.bpftime/bpftimetool add path path=$path: /.bpftime/ reference dump all (programs, maps, links) file: example: state.json [info] global shm constructed. open type 1 maps bpf map handler name=.rodata.str1.1 found 3 find prog fd=4 name=do uprobe trace perf event 5 file contains: program bytecode metadata definitions attributes attachments descriptors use cases: debugging runtime before modifications analysis reverse engineering sharing test cases load memory: spdlog level=debug minimal.json {"attr":{...},"name":".rodata.str1.1","type":"bpf handler"} 4 restore previous pre configured environments testing ci/cd pipelines cross deployment execute benchmark measurement: [repeat [type type] arguments: data input (program context) options: repeat times report average (default: 1) execution mode: jit aot interpret jit) examples: once input.bin 10000 iterations compare modes 1000 output: running time taken: 1250 return value: 0 modes: jit: llvm compilation (fast compilation, good performance) aot: compiled native code (best performance, requires prior compilation) interpret: interpreter (slowest, overhead) clean wide: warning: this destroys loaded programs, processes using bpftime. crashes reset environment

documents.title: bpftime-aot
documents.description: Ahead-of-Time (AOT) compiler for eBPF programs that converts eBPF bytecode to native machine code using LLVM.
documents.titleText: bpftime-aot
documents.descriptionText: ahead-of-time (aot) compiler for ebpf programs that converts ebpf bytecode to native machine code using llvm. ahead-of-time (aot) compiler for ebpf programs that converts ebpf bytecode to native machine code using llvm.
documents.bodyTerms: ahead time (aot) compiler for ebpf programs that converts bytecode native machine code using llvm. overview bpftime aot command line tool compiles elf objects high performance execution userspace. supports: compilation from x86/arm multiple input sources files shared memory standalone compiled helper function relocation seamless integration llvm emission debugging and optimization analysis the underlying library, see llvmbpf. installation after building bpftime, available at: /.bpftime/bpftime add path export path=$path: /.bpftime/ usage help] version] {build,compile,load,run} subcommands: build elf(s) object file compile loaded load into run execute program reference code: output dir] options: dir directory (default: current directory) emit instead example: all example/uprobe.bpf.o output/ generate each will produce separate (e.g., uprobe.o). already memory: example full workflow: 1. preload=build/runtime/syscall server/libbpftime syscall server.so example/malloc/malloc 2. output: count.o (native object) key advantage: when compiling memory, maps, global variables, functions are relocated, making ready integration. pre execution: arguments: update 4 directly: [memory] optional: context uprobe trace.o with program.o memory.bin examples linking custom you can link your c/c++ applications: tools/aot/example clang o2 main.c malloc ./malloc driver needs implement required functions: include entry point called main int bpf main(void ctx, uint64 size); implementations ext 0006(uint64 fmt, fmt size, ...) printk implementation list

documents.title: Userspace eBPF VM with LLVM JIT/AOT Compiler
documents.description: ![Build and Test VM](https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/llvmbpf/actions/workflows/test-vm.yml) ![codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/eunomia-bpf/llvmbpf)
documents.titleText: userspace ebpf vm with llvm jit/aot compiler
documents.descriptionText: ![build and test vm](https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/llvmbpf/actions/workflows/test-vm.yml) ![codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/eunomia-bpf/llvmbpf) ![build and test vm](https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/llvmbpf/actions/workflows/test-vm.yml) ![codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/eunomia-bpf/llvmbpf)
documents.bodyTerms: build and test codecov high performance, multi architecture jit/aot compiler virtual machine (vm) based llvm. this component part the bpftime project but focuses solely core vm. offers following capabilities: operates standalone ebpf library tool. compiles bytecode into llvm files. elf files aoted native code object files, which can linked like compiled objects loaded llvmbpf. loads executes aot within runtime. supports helpers maps lddw functions. optimized for flexibility, minimal dependencies. does not include implement, helpers, verifiers, loaders applications, making suitable lightweight, performance library. comprehensive userspace runtime that includes support maps, seamless execution uprobe, syscall trace, xdp, other programs—similar kernel functionality userspace—please refer project. with usage use llvmbpf load from file data relocation binary deployment optimizaion inline helper function original unit bpf conformance license sudo apt install 15 dev libzstd cmake dcmake type=release target all see example how vm: void run prog(const code, size len) uint64 res 0; vm; vm.load code(code, len); (res) return; vm.register external function(2, "print", (void )ffi print func); auto func vm.compile(); (!func) int err vm.exec(&bpf mem, sizeof(bpf mem), res); (err 0) printf("res priu64 "\n", cli: get libelf1 libelf dbuild cli=1 you cli generate bytecode: ./build/cli/bpftime .github/assets/sum.bpf.o emit test.bpf.ll opt o3

documents.title: EIM: Extension Interface Model Specification
documents.description: EIM is a framework for describing fine-grained interactions between extensions (plugins) and a host application. Its central principle is the principle of least privilege: each extension should receive only the minimal r
documents.titleText: eim: extension interface model specification
documents.descriptionText: eim is a framework for describing fine-grained interactions between extensions (plugins) and a host application. its central principle is the principle of least privilege: each extension should receive only the minimal r eim is a framework for describing fine-grained interactions between extensions (plugins) and a host application. its central principle is the principle of least privilege: each extension should receive only the minimal r
documents.bodyTerms: 1. overview eim framework for describing fine grained interactions between extensions (plugins) and host application. its central principle the least privilege each extension should receive only minimal resources required fulfill task. addresses two competing needs: interconnectedness may require access specific functions global state host. 2. safety want ensure that buggy can’t crash system cause security issues. 1.1. background: why eim? historically, frameworks have faced problem letting users interesting things while not endangering traditional approaches (like all read/write everything host) can lead to: bugs single might entire application unexpected side effects. incidents corrupt memory call privileged functions, compromise avoids these pitfalls precisely enumerating capabilities. this also has benefits maintenance auditing: clear separation developer defines possible points once. reusable administrators add remove capabilities deployment spec without changing host’s code. auditable specification document auditors review see which are actually granted extension. 1.2. key ideas any “thing” underlying need use—like cpu cycles, memory, variables, function calls. grant permission use given resource (e.g., right read variable, particular function, consume certain amount instructions). 3. entries hooks override extended. instance, allow hooking foo(), “request received” phase webserver. codifies, detail, provide (development time) how those allocated entry (deployment time). concepts

documents.title: UProbe Role Definition README
documents.description: - We have a set of host APIs (BPF helpers) related to uprobe usage available. - We define capabilities for these APIs. - We assign attributes that control resource and operational constraints. - We treat the uprobe in us
documents.titleText: uprobe role definition readme
documents.descriptionText: - we have a set of host apis (bpf helpers) related to uprobe usage available. - we define capabilities for these apis. - we assign attributes that control resource and operational constraints. - we treat the uprobe in us - we have a set of host apis (bpf helpers) related to uprobe usage available. - we define capabilities for these apis. - we assign attributes that control resource and operational constraints. - we treat the uprobe in us
documents.bodyTerms: this example assumes: have set host apis (bpf helpers) related uprobe usage available. define capabilities for these apis. assign attributes that control resource and operational constraints. treat the userspace ebpf specific role grants access relevant introduction model, defines which (and thus apis) extension can access. consider uprobes one such role, need define: 1. example: map helpers: bpf lookup elem, update delete elem string strncpy, strnlen, strcmp memory probe read, read str info get current pid tgid (or hypothetical helpers retrieve process/thread info) 2. group into logically coherent sets reflect common patterns. instance: maphelperscap operations stringhelperscap probehelperscap reading retrieving 3. behavioral constraints running uprobes, as: max (limit usage) instructions execution time per event) allow write (does modifications memory? usually not) always const, may side effect, skip verification (behavioral depending trust level) composing setting attributes, create uproberole extensions assume safely predictably perform logic. definition (conceptual) types: name: "const char ptr" base: "char constraints: ["non null", "null terminated"] "int positive" "int" ["value 0"] functions: "bpf elem" signature: params: {type: "void ptr", "map"} void "key"} return type: function "map.non null" "key.non "value"} "int", "flags"} "value.non strncpy" "dest"} "src"} "size t", "len"} "dest.non "src.non strnlen" "str"} "maxlen"}

documents.title: studies on bugs in extensions/plugins
documents.description: We have done several studies on bugs in extensions/plugins:
documents.titleText: studies on bugs in extensions/plugins
documents.descriptionText: we have done several studies on bugs in extensions/plugins: we have done several studies on bugs in extensions/plugins:
documents.bodyTerms: have done several studies bugs extensions/plugins: 1. browser extensions and ide plugins 2. database web server 3. hypervisor container 4. productivity software game extensions/plugins each study included around 20 real world cases, documenting common patterns impacts statistics root causes summary findings across extension ecosystems diverse environments, similar vulnerabilities despite targeting different host platforms— browsers ides engines hypervisor/container orchestration —the plugin model consistently introduces comparable classes bugs. all three domains, often run with broad privileges, interact untrusted data, integrate deeply the application’s internals. result, logic flaws, memory errors, security oversights can lead critical vulnerabilities, including: remote code execution (rce) that allow file manipulation, script injection, direct system calls exploited arbitrary code. for instance, malicious feature (e.g., rewriting scripts) vcenter open upload endpoint directly led rce. privilege escalation policy bypass add ons such kubernetes admission controllers inadvertently grant higher privileges than intended. multiple cases showed how permission checks enabled low privileged unauthenticated attackers escalate policies, hijack sensitive operations. safety lower level systems—like modules (apache, nginx) (mysql, redis) drivers (libvirt, xen) —heap/buffer overflows, use after free other mismanagement crashes, data corruption, this matches well known industry issues dominate c/c++ converge cause patterns: insufficient input validation

documents.title: Study on Bugs in Database and Web Server Extension Interfaces
documents.description: Database systems and web servers often support extension interfaces – plug-in modules or scripting engines that extend core functionality (e.g. PostgreSQL extensions, MySQL plugins/UDFs, Apache/Nginx modules, IIS ISAPI).
documents.titleText: study on bugs in database and web server extension interfaces
documents.descriptionText: database systems and web servers often support extension interfaces – plug-in modules or scripting engines that extend core functionality (e.g. postgresql extensions, mysql plugins/udfs, apache/nginx modules, iis isapi). database systems and web servers often support extension interfaces – plug-in modules or scripting engines that extend core functionality (e.g. postgresql extensions, mysql plugins/udfs, apache/nginx modules, iis isapi).
documents.bodyTerms: database systems and web servers often support extension interfaces plug modules scripting engines that extend core functionality (e.g. postgresql extensions, mysql plugins/udfs, apache/nginx modules, iis isapi). while powerful, these extensions have historically introduced numerous bugs ranging from critical security vulnerabilities logic performance issues. this study, examine both historical recent cases such server including flaws (buffer overflows, privilege escalations, injection bugs) well general (logic errors, bottlenecks, compatibility problems). present 20+ real world structured format, then analyze common patterns, impacts stability/maintainability, the frequency/severity different bug types. case studies table below documents selection notable affecting databases (postgresql, mysql, redis, mongodb, etc.) (apache httpd, nginx, microsoft iis). each includes identifier (cve issue), affected software/module, classification type, root cause, impact host system, resolution mitigation. cve software (extension) type cause mitigation 2023 39417 (extension scripts) improper quoting install script allowed sql (postgresql: 39417: @substitutions@ within allow injection) attacker can execute arbitrary code superuser patched block unsafe substitutions 2022 2625 used create replace non object flaw) 2625: scripts objects not belonging extension) malicious replacement could run another role (even superuser) change prevent replacing out 32305 (aiven extras missing schema qualification privileged functions (name collision) (nvd 32305) low priv user

documents.title: AgentSight: Zero-Instrumentation LLM Agent Observability with eBPF
documents.description: ![License: MIT](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT) ![Build Status](https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/agentsight)
documents.titleText: agentsight: zero-instrumentation llm agent observability with ebpf
documents.descriptionText: ![license: mit](https://opensource.org/licenses/mit) ![build status](https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/agentsight) ![license: mit](https://opensource.org/licenses/mit) ![build status](https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/agentsight)
documents.bodyTerms: license: mit build status agentsight observability tool designed specifically for monitoring llm agent behavior through ssl/tls traffic interception and process monitoring. unlike traditional application level instrumentation, observes the system boundary using ebpf technology, providing tamper resistant insights into interactions with minimal performance overhead. zero instrumentation required code changes, new dependencies, sdks. works any framework out box. github: arxiv: quick start wget https://github.com/eunomia bpf/agentsight/releases/download/v0.1.1/agentsight chmod record from claude sudo ./agentsight "claude" gemini cli (comm "node") "node" python tools "python" activity nvm node.js, bundle openssl statically binary path /usr/bin/node node visit http://127.0.0.1:8080 view recorded data. real time tree visualization showing file operations timeline calls captured data time. why agentsight? vs. challenge solution adoption sdk/proxy each drop daemon, changes closed source limited visibility complete prompts behaviors dynamic logs can silenced manipulated kernel hooks, encrypted only sees wrapper outputs captures unencrypted requests/responses misses subprocess executions tracks all multi systems isolated per tracing global correlation analysis critical that miss: bypass raw payloads before processing resource access cross communications coordination 🏗️ architecture ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ runtime ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ (langsmith, helicone, langfuse, etc.) vulnerable └─────────────────────────────────────────┘ (can bypassed) ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ (tamper proof) ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────────┐ ssl events └─────────────────┘ └─────────────────────┘ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ rust streaming ┌─────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌────────┐

documents.titleText: mcptrace: ai-powered kernel debugging with bpftrace
documents.descriptionText: enable ai assistants to debug linux kernel issues through natural language using bpftrace mcp server. no ebpf expertise required. a minimal mcp (model context protocol) server that provides ai assistants with access to bpftrace kernel tracing capabilities.
documents.bodyTerms: minimal mcp (model context protocol) server that provides assistants with access bpftrace kernel tracing capabilities. github repository https://github.com/eunomia bpf/mcptrace now implemented rust using the rmcp crate for better performance and type safety. python implementation still available git history. !bpftrace demo features powered debugging enable help you debug complex linux issues through natural language ebpf expertise required discover system trace points browse search thousands probes find exactly what need monitor from calls network packets rich examples curated collection production ready scripts common scenarios like bottlenecks, security monitoring, troubleshooting secure execution model run traces safely without giving direct root mcptrace acts gateway proper authentication asynchronous operation start long running retrieve results later perfect monitoring occur intermittently capability detection automatically your supports, including helpers, map types, probe types why mcptrace? traditionally requires deep expertise. changes that. bridging (the language), lets conversation. just describe want observe "show which processes are opening files" "trace slow disk operations" let generate appropriate traces. never gets access. gateway, its example information, has everything needed understand what's happening inside kernel. required. installation prerequisites 1. install (if not already installed): curl proto '=https' tlsv1.2 ssf https://sh.rustup.rs 2. ensure installed: sudo apt get

documents.title: spec_core
documents.description: The Extension Interface Model (EIM) is a specification framework that defines how a host environment can describe, verify, and regulate extensions—code modules that integrate with a host application or system to provide
documents.titleText: spec_core
documents.descriptionText: the extension interface model (eim) is a specification framework that defines how a host environment can describe, verify, and regulate extensions—code modules that integrate with a host application or system to provide the extension interface model (eim) is a specification framework that defines how a host environment can describe, verify, and regulate extensions—code modules that integrate with a host application or system to provide
documents.bodyTerms: extension interface model (eim) v2 1. introduction the specification framework that defines how host environment can describe, verify, and regulate extensions —code modules integrate with application system provide additional functionality. such may introduced various stages, including compile time, link load even dynamically runtime. eim ensures each operates within rigorously defined boundaries, adheres declared safety constraints, invokes only authorized provided interfaces. specifying types, capabilities, points (entries), imposes least privilege enables safe integration without sacrificing flexibility expressiveness. independent any particular runtime technology programming language: implementers define enforce specifications using different verification mechanisms execution environments. 2. key concepts types typed parameters return values, constrained conditions ensuring usage (e.g., non null, resource limited). exported entries (extension functions) functions exposed call into. they explicit contracts for behavior, preconditions, postconditions. (helpers) interfaces selectively available extensions. helpers grant controlled access resources must explicitly authorized. capabilities named permission bundles group sets allowed impose associated enabling fine grained control over what do. constraints logical attached ensure extension’s behavior remains safe, correct, predictable. designers: not one size fits all solution but rather customizable blueprint tuned balance safety, performance, designers their own according specific domain risk profile. 3. normative references terms "must", "must

documents.bodyTerms: overview the extension permission and control model (eim) provides structured, formally verifiable framework behavior dynamically loaded extensions within host environment. eim uses roles capabilities typed interfaces attributes define what operations may perform, how perform them, under resource constraints. key highlights: 1. assign bundles extensions. 2. sets allowed apis with well defined type signatures 3. impose quantitative qualitative operational limits. 4. ensure that every function adheres specified constraints, enabling static analysis formal verification. 5. inheritance composition support scalable, modular configuration permissions attributes. 6. verification its configurations are designed for verifier checks correctness, consistency, adherence constraints load time during pre deployment phase. language definitions basic domains let finite set (host provided functions). exported functions (extension entry points). types each associated properties. applicable roles, capabilities, can used check compatibility applicability between roles. introduce specification represented yaml/json domain specific (dsl). semantics remain same. definition (type): (basetype, constraints), where: basetype primitive (e.g., int, size char composite struct) type. logical predicates over values examples: non null pointer: value bounded integer: 0 100 terminated string: value[i] '\0' until terminating found system requirements: constraints(τ) must decidable predicates, allowing symbolic check, call, arguments return respect these example definitions: types: name: "const

(helpers) callable subject verification constraints. capabilities set grouped with permission bundles. roles plus constraints govern their indirectly control conditions resource limits, operational restrictions, every level model. these concepts collectively enable principle least privilege approach formal safety. 2. normative references language used herein ("must", "must not", "should", "should not") rfc 2119. unless otherwise noted, all statements are requirements. 3. terms definitions dynamically loaded can hook points, analogous ebpf program plugin. environment system runtime loads provides (helpers). 4. 4.1 definition: type associates base (e.g., int, char values must satisfy. (type constraints) include properties non null, null terminated, value 0. definition (type): (basetype, constraints(τ)), where: basetype primitive composite size struct). constraints(τ) finite logical predicates hold any bounded integer, terminated). requirements: each decidable, enabling static symbolic verification. verifier checks arguments return (hooks helpers) adhere declared example: types: name: "int positive" base: "int"

documents.title: Project architecture
documents.description: we have a loader library, a compile toolchain, and some additional tools like cli and a custom metrics exporter.
documents.titleText: project architecture
documents.descriptionText: we have a loader library, a compile toolchain, and some additional tools like cli and a custom metrics exporter. we have a loader library, a compile toolchain, and some additional tools like cli and a custom metrics exporter.
documents.bodyTerms: have loader library, compile toolchain, and some additional tools like cli custom metrics exporter. !eunomia arch.png bpf library wrapper main functions libbpf rs, provide the ability dynamically load ebpf code kernel run with simple json few api. see for details. interface provided which you can use start any program from url command. download release. examples more examples. operate wasm module eunomia module, write process data user space runtime. idea simple: 1. skeleton format toolchain 2. embed api operating 3. multiple single module. runtime fact, only exports vm, replace your own easily. example, locally built ecli: sudo ./ecli ./app.wasm also generate template build compiler container: x86 64 aarch64 docker pwd/:/src/ ghcr.io/eunomia bpf/ecc uname m:latest gen skel app sigsnoop example detail. help pre compiled used one command: toolchains repo github template. its publish workflow compiles src/package.json publishes release asset. other related projects lmp hub: github.com/linuxkerneltravel/lmp package manager based modules bolipi online runner: https://bolipi.com/ebpf/home/online runner newbies observability tool prometheus opentelemetry exporter metrics, written async rust: bpf-loader-rs

documents.title: benchmark
documents.description: Most of the time, bpf-loader work as a library to load eBPF program. It will not affect eBPF program after the program has been loaded and attached.
documents.titleText: benchmark
documents.descriptionText: most of the time, bpf-loader work as a library to load ebpf program. it will not affect ebpf program after the program has been loaded and attached. most of the time, bpf-loader work as a library to load ebpf program. it will not affect ebpf program after the program has been loaded and attached.
documents.bodyTerms: most the time, bpf loader work library load ebpf program. will not affect program after has been loaded and attached. todo: add more benchmark results for loading with exporter take opensnoop from bcc/libbpf tools example. starting bcc, you need about 0.8s start attach probe. out implement, only 50 70ms which significantly faster. aux grep eunomia root 171562 0.0 15176 4576 pts/6 01:08 0:00 sudo ./eunomia 171605 0.1 350540 7740 sl+ memory usage cpu also low. see details.

documents.title: ecc docker usage
documents.description: ``shell yourprogram.bpf.c yourprogram.h optional, if you want to use ring buffer to export events ``
documents.titleText: ecc docker usage
documents.descriptionText: ``shell yourprogram.bpf.c yourprogram.h optional, if you want to use ring buffer to export events `` ``shell yourprogram.bpf.c yourprogram.h optional, if you want to use ring buffer to export events ``
documents.bodyTerms: docker usage the only file you will need write is: your program.bpf.c program.h optional, want use ring buffer export events after that, simply run this: /path/to/repo/:/src ghcr.io/eunomia bpf/ecc uname m:latest absolute path get package.json root dir. just run: sudo ./ecli start can download ecli tool from eunomia bpf/releases, have pre build binaries for linux x86. small and dependencies, besides glibc glibcxx. wget https://aka.pw/bpf chmod ebpf compiled code different kernel versions(co re). see: github.com/eunomia bpf/eunomia bpf details. container image /path/to/repo:/src that without container, which listed below: github actions template compile online. template's publish.yml workflow compiles src/package.json publishes release asset. three steps: 1. this repo template: see creating repository 2. modify src/template.bpf.c, commit wait stop 3. generated latest fork, then notifications same libbpf, most libbpf any modification. supported program types: kprobe, tracepoint, fentry, add more types future. events, repo, define data type it, should struct, example: struct process event int pid; ppid; unsigned exit code; long duration ns; char comm[task comm len]; filename[max filename event; name field are not limited, but prefer standard types. multiple exists header, first one. feature enabled found map ringbuf program.

documents.titleText: ecc usage
documents.descriptionText: ``sh ecc [options] <sourcepath [exporteventheader] `` ``sh ecc [options] <sourcepath [exporteventheader] ``
documents.bodyTerms: ecc [options] [export event header] compiles and generates bpf object from the provided source path for specified ebpf program. example package.json only foo.bpf.c file header bar.h generate custom btfhub archive package into tar client.bpf.c event.h this command will compile client.bpf.o, them json, export containing btf file. output path: client.tar include files support things that make portable. provide information linux distributions released kernels don't have embedded information. using programs file, makes possible run older not support. list existing their current status arguments options bpf.c header: bpf.h defining struct workspace specify additional cflags cflags: clang a=" fstack protector", avoids runtime errors some stack protection enabled default. bin bin: binary (default: clang) llvm strip strip) subskeleton: pack config verbose: print execution yaml: skel yaml only: definition wasm btfgen: fetch does exist, clone archive. option take lot time, you want all files, can keep required worry, even archives 5 10mb size. archive: directory save (default:$home/.eunomia/btfhub archive) help: prints help documentation. version: version btfhub-archive

documents.bodyTerms: example this adapted version opensnoop from bcc/libbpf tools, you can check our source code here: examples/bpftools/opensnoop compile the locally like this: docker run /userpath/eunomia bpf/examples/bpftools/opensnoop:/src ghcr.io/eunomia bpf/ecc uname m:latest userpath needs replaced with your own repo path. after ebpf code, define config file programs: name: metrics: counters: eunomia open counter description: test labels: pid comm filename from: fname compiled filename: package.json use path file. find config.yaml. then, start exporter: config.yaml exporter sudo ./eunomia running program takes 46 listening http://127.0.0.1:8526 and waiting for events perf event... receiving request /metrics different bcc exporter, only thing need deployment machine package.json. there install llvm/clang bcc. result is: !prometheus manage tracing via api web api: curl post http://127.0.0.1:8526/start "content type: application/json" @examples/opensnoop/curl example.json {"id":1} see body. list all http://127.0.0.1:8526/list [{"id":0,"name":"bootstrap"},{"id":1,"name":"opensnoop"}] stop program: http://127.0.0.1:8526/stop '{"id": 1}' documents: cargo build release target/release/eunomia 0.1.0 usage: [options] options: sets custom [default: config.yaml] help print information

documents.title: manual
documents.description: 传统来说， eBPF 的开发方式主要有 BCC、libbpf 等方式。要完成一个 BPF 二进制程序的开发，需要搭建开发编译环境，要关注目标系统的内核版本情况，需要掌握从 BPF 内核态到用户态程序的编写，以及如何加载、绑定至对应的 HOOK 点等待事件触发，最后再对输出的日志及数据进行处理。
documents.titleText: manual
documents.descriptionText: 传统来说， ebpf 的开发方式主要有 bcc、libbpf 等方式。要完成一个 bpf 二进制程序的开发，需要搭建开发编译环境，要关注目标系统的内核版本情况，需要掌握从 bpf 内核态到用户态程序的编写，以及如何加载、绑定至对应的 hook 点等待事件触发，最后再对输出的日志及数据进行处理。 传统来说， ebpf 的开发方式主要有 bcc、libbpf 等方式。要完成一个 bpf 二进制程序的开发，需要搭建开发编译环境，要关注目标系统的内核版本情况，需要掌握从 bpf 内核态到用户态程序的编写，以及如何加载、绑定至对应的 hook 点等待事件触发，最后再对输出的日志及数据进行处理。
documents.bodyTerms: 传统来说， ebpf 的开发方式主要有 bcc、libbpf 等方式。要完成一个 bpf 二进制程序的开发，需要搭建开发编译环境，要关注目标系统的内核版本情况，需要掌握从 内核态到用户态程序的编写，以及如何加载、绑定至对应的 hook 点等待事件触发，最后再对输出的日志及数据进行处理。 我们希望有这样一种 的编译和运行工具链，就像其他很多语言一样： 大多数用户只需要关注 bpf.c 程序本身的编写，不需要写任何其他的什么 python, clang 之类的用户态辅助代码框架； 这样我们可以很方便地分发、重用 程序本身，而不需要和某种或几种语言的生态绑定； 最大程度上和主流的 libbpf 框架实现兼容，原先使用 框架编写的代码几乎不需要改动即可移植；eunomia 编写的 程序也可以使用 框架来直接编译运行； 本地只需要下载一个很小的二进制运行时，没有任何的 llvm 之类的大型依赖，可以支持热插拔、热更新； 也可以作为 lua 虚拟机那样的小模块直接编译嵌入其他的大型软件中，提供 程序本身的服务；运行和启动时资源占用率都很低； 程序的分发和使用像网页和 web 服务一样自然（make service）： 支持在集群环境中直接通过一次请求进行分发和热更新，仅需数十 payload， include typedef int pid char license[] sec("license") "dual bsd/gpl"; sec("tp/syscalls/sys enter write") handle tp(void ctx) get current tgid() 32; printk("bpf triggered from %d.\n", pid); return 0; 假设它叫 hello.bpf.c，新建一个 /path/to/repo 的文件夹并且把它放进去，接下来的步骤： 下载安装 ecli 二进制 wget https://aka.pw/bpf /usr/local/ecli chmod 使用容器进行编译，生成一个 package.json 文件，里面是已经编译好的代码和一些辅助信息 docker run /path/to/repo:/src ghcr.io/eunomia bpf/ecc uname m:latest 程序（root shell） sudo 的时候需要把包含 .bpf.c 文件的目录挂载到容器的 /src 目录下，目录中只有一个 文件； 它会追踪所有进行 write 系统调用的进程的 pid： cat /sys/kernel/debug/tracing/trace pipe 42755 [003] d...1 48755.529860: trace printk: 42755. 48755.529874: 我们编译好的 代码同样可以适配多种内核版本，可以直接把 复制到另外一个机器上，然后不需要重新编译就可以直接运行（co re：compile once every where）；也可以通过网络传输和分发 package.json，通常情况下，压缩后的版本只有几 到几十 kb。 map 记录数据 参考： struct uint(type, type hash); uint(max entries, 8192); type(key, t); type(value, u64); exec start sec(".maps"); 的功能和 没有任何区别，只需要在 中定义即可。 ring buffer 往用户态发送数据 只需要定义一个头文件，包含你想要发送给用户态的数据格式，以 作为后缀名： spdx license identifier: (lgpl 2.1 bsd 2 clause) copyright (c) 2020 facebook ifndef bootstrap define task comm len 16 max filename 127 event pid; ppid; unsigned exit code; long duration ns; comm[task len]; filename[max event; endif 在代码中定义环形缓冲区之后，就可以直接使用它： ringbuf); 256 1024); sec("tp/sched/sched process exec") exec(struct raw sched

documents.title: manual / 从 C 语言 的 Hello World 开始
documents.description: 传统来说， eBPF 的开发方式主要有 BCC、libbpf 等方式。要完成一个 BPF 二进制程序的开发，需要搭建开发编译环境，要关注目标系统的内核版本情况，需要掌握从 BPF 内核态到用户态程序的编写，以及如何加载、绑定至对应的 HOOK 点等待事件触发，最后再对输出的日志及数据进行处理。

documents.descriptionText: 传统来说， ebpf 的开发方式主要有 bcc、libbpf 等方式。要完成一个 bpf 二进制程序的开发，需要搭建开发编译环境，要关注目标系统的内核版本情况，需要掌握从 bpf 内核态到用户态程序的编写，以及如何加载、绑定至对应的 hook 点等待事件触发，最后再对输出的日志及数据进行处理。 构建用户态的 wasm 代码，并获取内核态数据

documents.title: Online Demo
documents.description: 可使用 bolipi 提供的在线体验服务，在线编译，在线运行、在线获取可视化结果：https://bolipi.com/ebpf/home/online
documents.titleText: online demo
documents.descriptionText: 可使用 bolipi 提供的在线体验服务，在线编译，在线运行、在线获取可视化结果：https://bolipi.com/ebpf/home/online 可使用 bolipi 提供的在线体验服务，在线编译，在线运行、在线获取可视化结果：https://bolipi.com/ebpf/home/online
documents.bodyTerms: 可使用 bolipi 提供的在线体验服务，在线编译，在线运行、在线获取可视化结果：https://bolipi.com/ebpf/home/online !imga 通过在线编译运行快速体验 ebpf 和可视化 本在线编译平台由 eunomia bpf 工具链提供支持，详细文档请参考 eunomia.dev/eunomia 在线编译 在代码编辑器中编写 的内核态程序，应当遵循 libbpf tools 的内核态代码编写约定，即： 代码编辑器 .bpf.c) 代码，它被编译成 package.json 头文件编辑器 .h) 可以选择包含通过 perf event 或环形缓冲区导出到用户空间的类型 我们目前只支持使用基于 的内核态代码，bcc 代码支持由于存在一些语法上的差异，还在开发中。 编写完成代码后，点击 按钮即可编译成 的内核态程序，在 编译输出 中查看编译输出： !imgb 更多信息请参考：eunomia 更多例子请参考：https://github.com/eunomia bpf/eunomia bpf/tree/master/examples/bpftools 在线运行 点击右侧的绿色运行按钮运行： !imgc 也可以通过 下载编译后的文件 查看编译好的程序，并在本地使用 ecli 直接运行： 下载安装 二进制 wget https://aka.pw/bpf ./ecli chmod 程序（root shell） sudo run prometheus 在线获取可视化结果 运行可视化组件 按钮，在弹出的窗口中配置 metrics 信息: !imgd 即可跳转到 界面，可通过选择 graph 查看可视化结果： 是一套编译工具链和运行时，以及一些附加项目，我们希望做到让 程序： 程序的编译和运行过程大大简化，抛去繁琐的用户态模板编写、繁琐的 bcc 安装流程，只需要编写内核态 程序，编译后即可在不同机器上任意内核版本下运行，并且轻松获取可视化结果。 真正像 javascript wasm 那样易于分发和运行，或者说内核态或可观测性层面的 faas：ebpf 即服务，通过 api 请求快速分发和运行，无需管理基础设施和用户态加载程序； eunomia-bpf

documents.bodyTerms: github template: eunomia bpf/eunomia template example bpf programs: examples/bpftools tutorial: bpf/bpf developer tutorial you can get pre compiled ebpf programs running from pages urls oci registry 1 line bash: download the latest release (aka.pw/bpf ecli redirects current asset) wget https://aka.pw/bpf chmod ./ecli sudo https://eunomia bpf.github.io/eunomia bpf/sigsnoop/package.json historical workflow, kept for compatibility run ghcr.io/eunomia bpf/execve:latest legacy remote http mode (ecli client server) has been removed main branch and archived archive/ecli http.

documents.title: build on android aarch64
documents.description: Android上build需要先Installing Debian on Android in Termux
documents.titleText: build on android aarch64
documents.descriptionText: android上build需要先installing debian on android in termux android上build需要先installing debian on android in termux
documents.bodyTerms: android上build需要先installing debian android termux 在android上构建时，需要先installing 1. install 2. proot distro select pkg login 3. packages remember first apk update apt clang cmake libelf1 libelf dev zlib1g 之后的步骤和在arm上build上相同 build arm eunomia bpf 到本地 git clone https://github.com/eunomia bpf/eunomia bpf.git submodule init recursive remote 配置环境变量 export config path=/usr/lib/aarch64 linux gnu/pkgconfig 安装依赖 4. 修改wasm runtime/cmakelists.txt wamr target（may not required） change set (wamr target "x86 64") "aarch64") 5. make loader ecli 6. 检查输出 root@localhost: /eunomia file ecli/build/bin/release/ecli ecli: elf 64 bit lsb pie executable, aarch64, version 1 (sysv), dynamically linked, interpreter /lib/ld aarch64.so.1, buildid[sha1]=eab42b79be75951e3a573aa7c61136239d35c868, for gnu/linux 3.7.0, with debug info, stripped

documents.bodyTerms: you want run the cli, most cases, don't need build your own. install dependencies will clang, libelf and zlib examples, package names may vary across distros. ubuntu/debian, need: apt clang libelf1 dev zlib1g centos/fedora, dnf elfutils devel rust toolchain curl https://sh.rustup.rs ssf bpf loader our core library written c++17. has other except libbpf. make ebpf recommended compiler gcc9 later. ecli after compiling loader, can cli tool c++: reference: wasm lib runtime please wasi sdk, download sdk release extract archive default path /opt/wasi compile code wasm. ecc from manager nix any distros with installed: github:eunomia bpf/eunomia ecli, see flake show for details application directly with: openeuler sudo search eunomia more check makefile project root details: refer info: ecc.yml bpf-loader-rs

documents.title: Install ecli and ecc
documents.description: - Install the ecli tool for running eBPF program from the cloud:
documents.titleText: install ecli and ecc
documents.descriptionText: - install the ecli tool for running ebpf program from the cloud: - install the ecli tool for running ebpf program from the cloud:
documents.bodyTerms: install the ecli tool for running ebpf program from cloud: wget https://aka.pw/bpf chmod ./ecli subcommands, including run, push, pull usage: [command line]... [command] .... ecc compiler toolchain compiling kernel code config file wasm module(clang, llvm, and libclang should installed compiling): https://github.com/eunomia bpf/eunomia bpf/releases/latest/download/ecc ./ecc eunomia bpf [options] [export event header] use docker image compile: x86 64 aarch64 run pwd/:/src/ ghcr.io/eunomia bpf/ecc uname m:latest compile with docker. pwd contains .bpf.c files files. build compiler, runtime library tools: see building details.

documents.title: supporting-external-BTF
documents.description: ``conf CONFIGDEBUGINFO=y CONFIGDEBUGINFOBTF=y ``
documents.titleText: supporting-external-btf
documents.descriptionText: ``conf configdebuginfo=y configdebuginfobtf=y `` ``conf configdebuginfo=y configdebuginfobtf=y ``
documents.bodyTerms: 1.确认内核btf选项已经打开 config debug info=y info btf=y 如果内核选项未开启，就需要重新编译内核 2. 确认当前内核btf信息是否可用，如不可用需添加外源btf信息 若/sys/kernel/btf/vmlinux存在，则btf可用，可通过bpftool生成vmlinux.h apt install linux tools (uname generic bpftool btf dump file /sys/kernel/btf/vmlinux format vmlinux.h 若/sys/kernel/btf/vmlinux不存在，则btf不可用，可通过btfhub添加外源btf信息或生成当前内核的定制化btf信息 （1）添加外源btf信息 btfgen tool create smaller files git clone git@github.com:aquasecurity/btfhub.git git@github.com:aquasecurity/btfhub archive.git btfhub //将克隆的归档文件放入 rsync avz ../btfhub archive/ exclude=.git exclude=readme.md ./archive/ 整个btfhub archive目录相当庞大，可以单独下载某个btf归档文件放入btfhub ../5.11.0 1027 azure.btf.tar.xz sending incremental list sent 77 bytes received 12 178.00 bytes/sec total size 144,419 speedup 1,622.69 之后就可以根据某个对应的ebpf 对象生成定制的btf 生成定制的 ebpf 对象的 文件: ./tools/btfgen.sh aarch64 $home/ .bpf.core.o 检查定制的新生成的 文件及其尺寸 find custom archive grep ubuntu tail 10 $ls lah archive/ubuntu/20.04/x86 64/5.8.0 1041 azure.btf （2）生成当前内核的定制化btf信息 sudo ./example static example file=5.8.0 63 generic.btf

(recommended) set sudo password export password="your password" ./autotune/target/release/autotune .mcp.json open cli tool

documents.bodyTerms: time required: 30 45 minutes difficulty: beginner prerequisites: basic c/c++ knowledge, nvidia gpu with cuda support the end this tutorial, you will understand how write, compile, and run complete program that performs parallel vector addition gpu. you'll learn fundamental workflow programming see real performance improvements over cpu only code. understanding challenge imagine need add two arrays 50,000 numbers together. cpu, would write loop processes one element time. sequential approach works, but it's slow when dealing large datasets. gpus excel type problem because they can process thousands elements simultaneously. think like this: having very fast worker, while workers who each handle small piece same for simple, repetitive tasks adding numbers, gpu's massive parallelism wins. getting started first, make sure have toolkit installed. verify your installation running: nvcc version smi first command shows compiler version, second displays information. both commands work, you're ready begin. clone tutorial repository navigate example: git https://github.com/eunomia bpf/basic building running let's start example action: 01 ./01 should output similar to: 50000 kernel launch 196 blocks 256 threads test passed done now what's happening under hood. model open addition.cu editor. every follows pattern: 1. allocate memory (host) (device) 2. copy input

documents.bodyTerms: time required: 45 60 minutes difficulty: intermediate prerequisites: completed tutorials 01 and 04, basic understanding neural networks the end this tutorial, you will understand how implement complete network forward pass gpu. you'll learn why are perfect for gpu acceleration, matrix multiplications activation functions efficiently, architectural from tutorial 04 applies real machine learning workloads. can find code gpus have revolutionized artificial intelligence, powering everything image recognition language translation. but modern billions parameters require trillions operations single pass. without gpus, training even running these would impossibly slow. consider what happens network: each layer, multiply input weight matrix, add biases, apply function. batch 64 images (28x28 pixels each) passing through layer with 128 neurons, need perform 784 6,422,528 operations. cpu processing one operation time, takes milliseconds. thousands cores working parallel, microseconds. almost embarrassingly parallel. output neuron's computation independent others. makes ideal fit architecture. building simple let's build run our representative networks: make 05 ./05 see like this: === example configuration: size: hidden 10 0.328 results (first 5 samples): sample 0 true label: 3, predicted: 1 probabilities: 0.0934 0.1879 0.0828 0.0640 0.0906 0.0844 0.1827 0.0417 0.0938 0.0788 1, 0.0955 0.1514 0.1014 0.0777 0.0916 0.0864 0.1401 0.0761

documents.descriptionText: time required: 60-75 minutes difficulty: intermediate to advanced prerequisites: completed tutorials 04 and 05, understanding of convolutions time required: 60-75 minutes difficulty: intermediate to advanced prerequisites: completed tutorials 04 and 05, understanding of convolutions
documents.bodyTerms: time required: 60 75 minutes difficulty: intermediate advanced prerequisites: completed tutorials 04 and 05, understanding convolutions the end this tutorial, you will understand how convolutional layers work gpu, why shared memory optimization crucial for performance, architectural principles from tutorial translate dramatic real world speedups. you'll see 30x performance improvement single technique. can find code dominate computer vision neural networks (cnns) have revolutionized vision. they power face recognition your phone, object detection self driving cars, medical image analysis. but cnns are computationally expensive forward pass through modern cnn require billions multiply add operations. convolution operation bottleneck. typical like resnet 50, over 95% computation spent layers. makes most important optimize when implementing gpus. unlike fully connected exploit spatial structure. instead connecting every input output, applies small filter (kernel) that slides across input. sliding window processes local regions, which perfect images where nearby pixels related. running example let's start seeing action: make 06 ./06 output this: === with configuration: size: 28x28 1 channels kernel 5x5 16 padding: 2, stride: size after convolution: pooling: 14x14 comparison: direct 0.223 0.008 speedup: 29.68x layer timings (shared memory): relu activation: 0.006 max verification: difference between implementations: 0.000000e+00 results match:

documents.bodyTerms: this document explores the boundary between cpu and gpu profiling, examining which operations can effectively measured side versus require instrumentation. we'll also discuss how advanced function hooking techniques like ebpf complement profiling. table contents 1. 2. measurable 3. 4. when kernel instrumentation necessary 5. functions with 6. integrated profiling approaches 7. case studies 8. future directions 9. references modern computing involves complex interplay host (cpu) device (gpu) operations. understanding where place depends what aspects performance you're measuring: ┌────────────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ application code execution └────────┬─────────┘ ┌────────▼─────────┐ cuda runtime warp scheduler ┌─────────┐ driver │◄─┼──┤pcie bus │────►│ memory controller └─────────┘ system software hardware └──────────────────┘ └────────────────────────┘ following from side: api call latency launch overhead time begins allocation spent cudamalloc, cudafree, etc. transfers duration cudamemcpy synchronization points cudadevicesynchronize, cudastreamsynchronize resource management usage tracking deallocation patterns stream creation creating destroying streams context switching contexts interaction frequency rate pattern calls wait spends waiting for i/o overlap interact utilization level metrics pcie traffic volume timing data transferred over power consumption wide correlated activity thermal effects temperature changes that may affect throttling tools measurement cupti callbacks hook into via interface binary pin dynamorio intercept interposing libraries custom linux's extended berkeley packet filter

documents.bodyTerms: you can find the code collection cuda programming examples learn gpu with nvidia cuda. make sure change architecture 61 your own makefile and tutorials 01 vector addition.cu addition.md: introduction addition example 02 ptx assembly.cu assembly.md: demonstration inline assembly multiplication 03 methods.cu methods.md: comprehensive comparison methods including cuda, ptx, thrust, unified memory, shared streams, dynamic parallelism using matrix 04 architecture.cu architecture.md: detailed exploration organization hierarchy hardware architecture, thread/block/grid structure, memory hierarchy, execution model 05 neural network.cu network.md: implementing basic network forward pass 06 cnn convolution.cu convolution.md: accelerated convolution operations for optimization 07 attention mechanism.cu mechanism.md: implementation mechanism transformer models 08 profiling tracing.cu tracing.md: tracing applications events, nvtx, cupti performance 09 extension.cu extension.md: application extension mechanisms modifying behavior without source changes, api interception, management, kernel optimization, error resilience 10 cpu boundaries.cu boundaries.md: advanced instrumentation techniques demonstrating fine grained internal timing, divergent path analysis, workload profiling, adaptive algorithm selection within kernels 11 modifications.cu modifications.md: customizations data structure layout warp level primitives, access patterns, fusion, 12 customizations.cu customizations.md: customization thread divergence mitigation, register usage mixed precision computation, persistent threads load balancing, specialization patterns 13 low latency packet processing.cu processing.md: minimizing based processing, pinned zero copy stream

documents.description: The GitHub repo and complete tutorial is available at <https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/cupti-tutorial.
documents.titleText: cupti asynchronous activity tracing tutorial
documents.descriptionText: the github repo and complete tutorial is available at <https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/cupti-tutorial. the github repo and complete tutorial is available at <https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/cupti-tutorial.
documents.bodyTerms: the github repo and complete tutorial available introduction profiling gpu applications can significantly impact performance not done carefully. this demonstrates how use cupti's asynchronous activity tracing collect data with minimal your application's execution time. you'll learn detailed api traces while application continues run full speed. what set buffer handling cupti techniques for non blocking collection processing information separate thread minimizing vs. synchronous before diving into code, let's understand why matters: blocks during running simple implementation requires safe higher lower suitable short test runs production long code walkthrough 1. setting key configuring management: void inittrace() enable activityattribute attr callbacks; bufferscallbackrequestfunc bufferrequested bufferrequestedcallback; bufferscallbackcompletefunc buffercompleted buffercompletedcallback; call(cuptiactivityregistercallbacks(bufferrequested, buffercompleted)); kinds you want track call(cuptiactivityenable(cupti kind device)); context)); driver)); runtime)); memcpy)); memset)); kernel)); concurrent capture timestamp normalizing times call(cuptigettimestamp(&starttimestamp)); 2. request callback similar version, but now must safe: static cuptiapi bufferrequestedcallback(uint8 buffer, size size, maxnumrecords) buf size; allocate extra space alignment (uint8 )malloc( align size); null) printf("error: out memory\n"); exit( 1); buffer( limit number records maxnumrecords 0; 3. completion most important part callback, which thread: buffercompletedcallback(cucontext ctx, uint32 streamid, uint8 validsize) cuptiresult status; record null; thread, safety essential process all status cuptiactivitygetnextrecord(buffer, validsize, &record); (status success)

documents.titleText: cupti activity trace tutorial
documents.descriptionText: the github repo and complete tutorial is available at <https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/cupti-tutorial. the github repo and complete tutorial is available at <https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/cupti-tutorial.
documents.bodyTerms: the github repo and complete tutorial available introduction profiling cuda applications essential for understanding their performance characteristics. cupti activity api provides powerful way collect detailed traces both calls gpu activities. this explains how use gather analyze data. what you'll learn initialize setting managing record buffers processing records from multiple sources interpreting data optimization code walkthrough 1. tracing core system revolves around buffer management. requests store notifies when those are filled. request callback called needs new static void cuptiapi bufferrequested(uint8 buffer, size size, maxnumrecords) allocate buf size; (uint8 )malloc( align size); ensure properly aligned buffer( maxnumrecords 0; function allocates memory records. alignment important performance. 2. completed completion has filled buffercompleted(cucontext ctx, uint32 streamid, uint8 validsize) null; process all cuptiresult status success; while (validsize 0) cuptiactivitygetnextrecord(buffer, validsize, &record); (status success) printactivity(record); validsize common.size; else break; free(buffer); fills with data, processes each then frees buffer. 3. printactivity heart analysis, different types activities: printactivity(cupti record) switch (record kind) case kind device: print device information ... memcpy: copy details kernel: kernel execution many more types... type insights: activities show hardware capabilities reveal transfer patterns times time parameters 4. initialization cleanup inittrace() register callbacks management call(cuptiactivityregistercallbacks(bufferrequested, buffercompleted)); enable

documents.bodyTerms: the github repo and complete tutorial available introduction cupti concurrent profiling sample demonstrates advanced techniques for complex cuda applications that use multiple streams, devices, threads. this shows how handle challenges gpu operations while maintaining accuracy minimizing overhead. what you'll learn profile with streams multi device analysis understanding concurrency patterns managing overhead high throughput scenarios correlating activities across different execution contexts presents unique challenges: 1. overlapping kernels memory transfers executing simultaneously 2. coordination synchronizing gpus 3. thread safety handling data from cpu threads 4. context management tracking 5. timeline correlation accurate timing relationships key concepts stream based kernel asynchronous api calls parallel peer cross synchronization making shared resources local building prerequisites toolkit system (recommended full functionality) c++11 compatible compiler build process make creates executable various scenarios. architecture test includes several patterns: single sequential baseline comparison workload distribution mixed workloads combination compute components class concurrentprofiler private: std::vector contexts; profilethreads; std::atomic profiling; threadsafedatacollector collector; public: void startprofiling(); profiledevice(int deviceid); collectstreammetrics(cudastream stream); generateconcurrencyreport(); running basic ./concurrent output === 0 analysis: total streams: 4 kernels: 8 utilization: 85.3% 1 6 78.1% metrics: overlap ratio: 0.73 transfer overlap: 0.89 bandwidth: 28.5 gb/s time: 45.2ms equivalent: 124.7ms speedup factor:

documents.bodyTerms: the github repo and complete tutorial available introduction cupti finalization sample demonstrates proper cleanup procedures techniques for based profiling applications. this covers best practices resource management, graceful shutdown procedures, ensuring data collection before application termination. what you'll learn how properly finalize sessions understanding requirements implementing reporting handling edge cases error conditions during crucial for: 1. integrity all collected flushed saved 2. releasing resources avoiding memory leaks 3. termination without loss 4. managing 5. performance impact minimizing overhead key steps activity buffer flush remaining records process pending activities close streams event group disable active groups destroy objects release associated callback deregistration unsubscribe from callbacks clean structures ensure context device contexts per building prerequisites cuda toolkit with integration management build make creates executable demonstrating procedures. running basic execution ./cupti output === starting session... 1500ms beginning process... finalization: flushing buffers... processed 2,847 cleanup: disabling 3 destroying deregistration: unsubscribing 5 domains cleaning 2 validation: leaks: 0 handles: operations: completed successfully 45ms to: results.json code architecture manager class cuptifinalizationmanager private: struct resourcetracker std::vector activeeventgroups; activecallbacks; managedcontexts; activitybuffers; bool isfinalized; resources; std::mutex finalizationmutex; std::atomic finalizationinprogress; public: void registereventgroup(cupti eventgroup group); registercallback(cupti subscriberhandle subscriber); registercontext(cucontext context); beginfinalization(); finalizeactivities(); finalizeeventgroups();

documents.bodyTerms: the github repo and complete tutorial available introduction nvtx (nvidia tools extension) provides powerful way annotate your cuda applications with custom ranges, markers, metadata. this sample demonstrates how integrate cupti capture analyze annotations, enabling better understanding application structure performance bottlenecks. what you'll learn instrument annotations creating domains for organized profiling using push/pop start/end range patterns registering strings naming resources integrating comprehensive analysis best practices instrumentation key concepts several annotation types: ranges mark duration code sections (push/pop start/end) markers point time events logical groupings categories classification messages descriptive text create domain vector operations nvtxdomainhandle nvtxdomaincreatea("vector addition"); use specific nvtxdomainrangepusha(domain, "memory allocation"); ... nvtxdomainrangepop(domain); architecture setup integration required environment variable linux: export injection64 path= /libcupti.so windows: set /cupti.dll include "nvtx3/nvtoolsext.h" "nvtx3/nvtoolsextcuda.h" "nvtx3/nvtoolsextcudart.h" "generated meta.h" callback void cuptiapi nvtxcallbackhandler(void userdata, callbackdomain domain, callbackid callbackid, const callbackdata callbackinfo); event attributes nvtxeventattributes eventattrib {0}; eventattrib.version version; eventattrib.size attrib struct size; eventattrib.messagetype message type ascii; eventattrib.colortype color argb; eventattrib.color 0x0000ff; blue eventattrib.message.ascii "custom operation"; walkthrough dovectoraddition() name identification cudevice device; cudeviceget(&device, 0); nvtxnamecudevicea(device, "cuda device 0"); configure main operation "vectoradd"; nvtxdomainrangepushex(domain, &eventattrib); phase 1: memory allocation default nvtxrangepusha("allocate host memory"); allocate phosta (int )malloc(size); phostb phostc nvtxrangepop(); 2:

", devicecount); get compute capability cudevice default first curesult err cudeviceget(&device, dev); (err success) printf("error: cudeviceget failed with error %d
", err); return; major 0, minor cudevicegetattribute(&major, attribute major, device); cudevicegetattribute(&minor, minor, printf("compute capability: %d.%d
", minor); enumerating group counters. all device: void enumeventdomains(cudevice device) number uint32 numdomains call(cuptidevicegetnumeventdomains(device, &numdomains)); printf("device has domains

", device, numdomains); (numdomains 0) printf("no found allocate space hold ids eventdomainid domainids (cupti )malloc(numdomains sizeof(cupti eventdomainid)); (domainids null) printf("failed memory ids
"); call(cuptideviceenumeventdomains(device, &numdomains, domainids)); domain, print information about (int getdomains displays supported specified getmetrics getevents step usage build sample: make 0: ./cupti 0

documents.bodyTerms: the github repo and complete tutorial available introduction when profiling cuda applications, you often need monitor performance metrics while your application running. this demonstrates how use cupti's event sampling capabilities collect gpu data regular intervals during kernel execution, giving real time insights into application's behavior. what you'll learn set continuous nvidia gpus techniques for monitoring events kernels are running creating multi threaded system interpreting sampled analysis understanding unlike one collection that gives single value end allows to: 1. change over execution 2. detect variations anomalies 3. correlate activity with specific phases algorithm 4. observe impact dynamic workloads code walkthrough setting thread core sample dedicated collects main runs computations: static void func(void arg) samplinginfo info (samplinginfo )arg; cucontext context context; cudevice device device; make same cuctxsetcurrent(context); want cupti eventgroup eventgroup; eventid eventid; get specified (default "inst executed") call(cuptieventgetidfromname(device, eventname, &eventid)); create group call(cuptieventgroupcreate(context, &eventgroup, 0)); add call(cuptieventgroupaddevent(eventgroup, eventid)); mode (critical execution) call(cuptieventgroupsetattribute(eventgroup, attr mode, sizeof(cupti eventcollectionmode), &continuous)); enable call(cuptieventgroupenable(eventgroup)); until computation done (!info terminate) read current size valuesize sizeof(uint64 t); uint64 eventvalue 0; call(cuptieventgroupreadevent(eventgroup, flag none, eventid, &valuesize, &eventvalue)); print printf("%s: %llu\n", (unsigned long long)eventvalue); wait before taking next millisleep(sample period ms); cleanup

documents.bodyTerms: the github repo and complete tutorial available introduction cupti nested range profiling sample demonstrates how implement hierarchical performance analysis using ranges. this technique allows you create detailed profiles with multiple levels granularity, enabling fine grained complex algorithms function calls. what you'll learn manage ranges implementing measurement understanding inheritance scope management analyzing algorithm patterns building tree structured reports provides several key advantages: 1. break down into constituent parts 2. based automatic raii principles 3. contextual data understand within phases 4. multi level granularity profile different detail simultaneously 5. call visualize execution concepts hierarchy structure: application ├── initialization memory allocation └── setup computation phase 1 kernel 2 transfer cleanup attributes each can have: name descriptive identifier category grouping mechanism (compute, memory, io) color visualization hint payload custom attached metrics counters specific child inherit properties from parents: metric collection configuration output formatting preferences error handling settings prerequisites cuda toolkit c++14 compatible compiler (for management) nvtx library for enhanced build process make creates executable demonstrating techniques. code architecture classes class profilerange private: std::string name; category; uint64 starttime; std::vector children; std::map metrics; public: profilerange(const std::string& name, const "default"); profilerange(); automatically ends createchild(const ""); void addmetric(const double value);

documents.titleText: cupti pc sampling tutorial
documents.descriptionText: the github repo and complete tutorial is available at <https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/cupti-tutorial. the github repo and complete tutorial is available at <https://github.com/eunomia-bpf/cupti-tutorial.
documents.bodyTerms: the github repo and complete tutorial available introduction when optimizing cuda kernels, it's essential know which parts your code consume most execution time. program counter (pc) sampling powerful technique that allows you identify hotspots gpu with minimal performance overhead. this demonstrates how use cupti's api collect samples during kernel map them back source code, helping focus optimization efforts where they'll have impact. what you'll learn configure enable for kernels collecting processing mapping addresses locations identifying analyzing data guide understanding works periodically recording current instruction being executed each active thread. process follows: 1. hardware warps 2. these are collected buffered 3. after completes, analyzed determine regions were frequently 4. mapped using debugging information unlike instrumentation based profiling, has impact provides statistical view spends its walkthrough setting first, need sampling: cupti pcsamplingconfig config; memset(&config, 0, sizeof(config)); config.size sizeof(config); config.samplingperiod 5; sample every 5 cycles config.samplingperiod2 0; not used example config.samplingperiodratio config.collectingmode collection mode kernel; config.samplingbuffersize 0x2000; 8kb buffer config.stoppingcount stopping condition get context cucontext context; driver call(cuctxgetcurrent(&context)); call(cuptipcsamplingenable(&config)); code: creates configuration structure sets period (how often pc) configures size storing 5. enables registering callbacks handle data, register will called buffers full: handling call(cuptipcsamplingregisterbufferhandler(handlebuffer, userdata));
